bytes.topic: Raspberry Pi — Linux on embedded, GPIO via Python/C, choosing Pi vs microcontroller
bytes.title: Arduino vs Raspberry Pi: Choosing Between Linux and Microcontroller
bytes.body: When deciding between Arduino and Raspberry Pi, consider the trade-offs between Linux and microcontroller simplicity. Arduino boards run on a lightweight C++ framework, ideal for rapid prototyping and IoT projects. In contrast, Raspberry Pi offers a full Linux distribution, enabling advanced development and customization. However, this comes at the cost of increased complexity and resource overhead. If your project requires intricate low-level control or real-time performance, Arduino's microcontroller-based approach may be more suitable. Conversely, if you need to leverage Linux's versatility and extensive libraries, Raspberry Pi might be the better choice.

bytes.topic: Embedded Fundamentals — microcontroller vs microprocessor, bare metal vs RTOS
bytes.title: Arduino - Microcontroller vs Microprocessor: Choosing the Right One for Your Project
bytes.body: Arduino boards are based on microcontrollers, which integrate the processor, memory, and peripherals on a single chip. This design provides low power consumption, reduced size, and increased reliability. In contrast, microprocessors are separate components that require external memory and peripherals. When working with Arduino, you'll typically use a microcontroller-based board. To illustrate the difference, consider the Arduino Uno, which features the ATmega328P microcontroller. This chip includes 32KB of flash memory, 2KB of SRAM, and various peripherals like timers and serial communication interfaces. For projects requiring advanced multitasking and real-time operating systems (RTOS), you might opt for an Arduino Due, which uses a microprocessor-based SAM3X8E chip.

bytes.topic: Zephyr RTOS — device tree, Kconfig, west tool, multi-platform support
bytes.title: Arduino Zephyr RTOS: Leverage Device Tree and Kconfig for Multi-Platform Support
bytes.body: Zephyr RTOS can be used on various Arduino boards due to its multi-platform support. To configure the board, create a `devicetree.dts` file that defines the board's peripherals. The `Kconfig` file is used to configure the Zephyr kernel. Use the `west` tool to build the Zephyr project. For example, to build for Arduino Nano 33 IoT, create a `west.yml` file with the board's configuration and then run `west build -b nano33_iot`. To use the device tree in Arduino, include the `ZEPHYR_BASE` environment variable in your project's `platform.txt` file, pointing to the Zephyr RTOS installation directory.

bytes.topic: Hardware Abstraction — GPIO, UART, SPI, I2C, PWM — what they are and when to use them
bytes.title: Arduino - Taming Hardware Chaos: GPIO, UART, SPI, I2C, PWM
bytes.body: Arduino's hardware abstraction layers simplify interacting with peripherals. GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) pins are used for digital input/output. UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter) for serial communication, SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface) for high-speed data transfer, and I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) for communication between ICs. PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) is used for analog output. Use GPIO for digital input/output, UART for serial communication, SPI for high-speed data transfer, I2C for IC communication, and PWM for analog output.

bytes.topic: Arduino — setup/loop model, libraries, shields, hardware vs software serial
bytes.title: Arduino — Mastering setup() and loop()
bytes.body: The setup() and loop() functions in Arduino are the core of any project. setup() is called once at the beginning, while loop() runs indefinitely. Use setup() for initialization and loop() for continuous tasks. Library functions can be called from both. Shields, such as Wi-Fi or Ethernet, often require manual initialization. Software serial communication can be used when hardware pins are already in use. Use Serial.begin() for hardware serial and SoftwareSerial.begin() for software serial.

bytes.topic: Interrupts — ISR design, interrupt priorities, avoiding blocking in ISRs
bytes.title: Arduino: Mastering Interrupts with ISR Design and Priorities
bytes.body: Arduino's interrupt service routine (ISR) allows for efficient handling of interrupts. However, ISRs should be concise and avoid blocking to prevent system freezes. Use the `attachInterrupt()` function to specify the interrupt priority and handler. For example, on Arduino Uno, pin change interrupts have a higher priority than external interrupts. To avoid blocking, use `while (digitalRead(PIN) == LOW)` instead of `while (true)` in the ISR to ensure a clean release of the interrupt.

bytes.topic: Real-time Constraints — deadlines, jitter, priority inversion, watchdog timers
bytes.title: Arduino: Mastering Real-time Constraints with Deadlines, Jitter, and Watchdog Timers
bytes.body: Arduino's real-time capabilities are crucial for applications like robotics, IoT, and automation. However, ensuring deterministic behavior can be challenging. Consider a scenario where a critical task must complete within a deadline. Arduino's `delay()` function introduces jitter, making it unsuitable for real-time tasks. Instead, use the `millis()` function to track time and set deadlines. Additionally, be aware of priority inversion, which occurs when a low-priority task blocks a high-priority task. Arduino's `interrupt()` function can help address this issue. Furthermore, watchdog timers can be used to detect and recover from system failures. To implement a watchdog timer, use the `Watchdog` library and set a timeout. If the timeout expires, the system will reset.

bytes.topic: ESP32 — dual-core, WiFi/BT built-in, FreeRTOS integration, power modes
bytes.title: Arduino ESP32 — Mastering Power Modes with FreeRTOS
bytes.body: To optimize power consumption on Arduino ESP32 boards, use the `ESP32DeepSleep` function in conjunction with FreeRTOS tasks. The `esp_deep_sleep_start()` API initiates a low-power mode that saves current while maintaining RTC functionality. For example, schedule a task to wake the board after a specified interval using `xTaskCreate()`. When waking up, use `esp_sleep_wake_reason_get()` to determine the cause. To enter power-down mode, call `esp_deep_sleep()` instead.

bytes.topic: Memory Constraints — RAM/flash limitations, stack sizing, heap fragmentation
bytes.title: Arduino: Mastering RAM/Flash Limitations
bytes.body: Arduino boards have limited RAM (2KB - 8KB) and flash memory (32KB - 1MB) due to their hardware constraints. To optimize performance, carefully manage the stack size and heap allocation. The Arduino IDE's `PROGMEM` keyword helps store data in flash memory, reducing RAM usage. However, improper usage can lead to stack overflows and crashes. A well-designed system should balance memory allocation with the `malloc` and `free` functions. When debugging memory issues, use `Serial.print` to inspect memory usage and identify potential bottlenecks.

bytes.topic: Power Management — sleep modes, wake sources, battery life optimization
bytes.title: Optimize Arduino Battery Life with Sleep Modes and Wake Sources
bytes.body: Arduino boards feature several sleep modes to conserve power, but they require precise configuration to balance energy savings with responsiveness. `brownOut` and `reset` are common wake sources, but they can be unreliable. Use `setSystemClock()` to switch to a lower-power clock, and `enableADC()` to disable the analog-to-digital converter during sleep. When waking, use `attachInterrupt()` to trigger a function on a specific pin.

bytes.topic: FreeRTOS — tasks, queues, semaphores, mutexes, interrupt service routines
Networking on Embedded — MQTT, CoAP, HTTP on constrained devices
bytes.title: MQTT, CoAP, and HTTP on Arduino: Choosing the Right Protocol
bytes.body: When connecting Arduino boards to the Internet, you have three primary options: MQTT, CoAP, and HTTP. MQTT and CoAP are designed for constrained devices, while HTTP is more commonly used for web servers. Arduino's WiFi library supports all three, but with varying degrees of complexity. For IoT applications, MQTT's publish-subscribe model is a good choice. CoAP's request-response model is suitable for resource-constrained devices. HTTP is the simplest option but may incur higher latency. Use the `WiFiClient` class for HTTP, `PubSubClient` for MQTT, and `CoapClient` for CoAP. For example, connecting to an MQTT server with `PubSubClient` is as simple as `PubSubClient client(wifi, port, username, password);`

bytes.topic: Peripheral Communication — reading sensors, driving actuators, protocol selection
bytes.title: Arduino: Mastering Analog and Digital I/O with Sensors and Actuators
bytes.body: When reading analog sensors, use `analogRead()` to convert ADC values to voltage, and `map()` to linearly scale values. For digital sensors, employ `digitalRead()` and `pinMode()` to set input modes. To drive actuators, utilize `analogWrite()` for PWM outputs and `digitalWrite()` for digital outputs. The choice between `I2C`, `SPI`, and `UART` protocols depends on the sensor or actuator's requirements. For instance, use `Wire` for I2C communication.

bytes.topic: Choosing a Platform — Arduino vs ESP32 vs Raspberry Pi vs STM32 decision framework
bytes.title: Arduino vs ESP32 vs Raspberry Pi vs STM32: Choosing the Right Microcontroller for Your Project
bytes.body: When selecting a microcontroller for your Arduino project, consider factors like processing power, memory, and I/O capabilities. Arduino boards offer a range of options, but you may need more power or specific features. ESP32 combines Wi-Fi and Bluetooth capabilities, while Raspberry Pi provides a Linux-based OS. STM32 series offer high-performance and large Flash memory. Evaluate your project's requirements and choose the board that best fits. For instance, if you need Wi-Fi and BLE connectivity, ESP32 might be the best choice.

bytes.topic: Bootloaders — what they do, OTA update mechanisms, secure boot
bytes.title: Arduino Bootloaders 101: OTA Updates and Secure Boot
bytes.body: Arduino boards rely on bootloaders to facilitate Over-The-Air (OTA) firmware updates. The Arduino IDE uses the `AVR109` protocol for uploading sketches to the board. However, for OTA updates, we use the `AVR109` bootloader alongside libraries like `WiFiClient` and `ArduinoOTA`. Secure boot is achieved through the `MK20DX256` bootloader's secure flash area, which stores the boot code. You can use `arduino-esp32` library to implement secure boot on ESP32 boards.

bytes.topic: Debugging Embedded — JTAG/SWD, logic analyzers, printf debugging limitations
bytes.title: Arduino Debugging - Beyond Serial Monitor
bytes.body: When the serial monitor isn't enough, Arduino developers turn to JTAG/SWD and logic analyzers. JTAG (Joint Test Action Group) is a standard for embedded systems debugging. Arduino boards like Due and Zero support JTAG using the ARM CMSIS-DAP protocol. SWD (Serial Wire Debug) is a variant of JTAG with a single wire for data transfer. The Arduino IDE provides an implementation of SWD debugging using the CMSIS-DAP protocol. Logic analyzers like Saleae or Bus Pirate can capture and display multiple signals in a system. However, using printf for debugging can be inefficient and may interfere with the system's operation.

bytes.topic: Testing Embedded — hardware-in-the-loop, unit testing on host, mocking hardware
bytes.title: Arduino - Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Testing for Embedded Systems

bytes.topic: Safety-Critical Systems — MISRA C, functional safety, certification considerations
bytes.title: Arduino Safety-Critical Systems: MISRA C Compliance and Certification
bytes.body:
Arduino boards running safety-critical systems must adhere to MISRA C guidelines to ensure functional safety. The Arduino IDE provides a MISRA C 2012 compliance mode, which can be enabled by setting the `compiler.misra2012` flag to `true` in the `platformio.ini` file. To write MISRA C compliant code, use the `Arduino::MISRA::_Static_assert` macro to verify integer overflow checks. For example:
```cpp
Arduino::MISRA::_Static_assert(sizeof(int) == 2, "Error: sizeof(int) not 2");
```
Certification for safety-critical systems typically requires a thorough audit of the code, including code reviews and static analysis. Arduino boards may be certified to relevant safety standards such as IEC 61508 or ISO 13849.

bytes.topic: FreeRTOS — tasks, queues, semaphores, mutexes, interrupt service routines
bytes.title: Arduino - Mastering FreeRTOS Tasks and Synchronization
bytes.body: FreeRTOS on Arduino boards like Arduino Due and Arduino Zero Pro offers a robust and efficient way to manage concurrent tasks and synchronization primitives. Tasks can be created and managed using the `taskCREATE()` function, while a queue can be used to pass data between tasks using the `QueueCreate()` function. Semaphores can be used for synchronization, and mutexes can be used for mutual exclusion. Interrupt service routines (ISRs) can be used to handle hardware interrupts. The `xTaskCreate()` function is used to create a task, while `xTaskDelete()` is used to delete a task. The `xSemaphoreTake()` function takes a semaphore, and `xSemaphoreGive()` gives it back.
